title: Revealing Hund superdispersion with tunneling spectroscopy
authors: Luke C. Rhodes, Fabian B. Kugler, Olivier Gingras, Carolina Marques, Edgar Abarca Morales, Phil D. C. King, Antoine Georges, Peter Wahl
abstract: In cuprate superconductors, electron-electron repulsion results in characteristic spectroscopic features known as `waterfalls', where the sharp quasiparticle dispersion transitions into broad Hubbard bands. However, in multi-orbital systems, the additional Hund coupling results in behavior that defies the conventional Mott--Hubbard paradigm, creating qualitatively distinct `superdispersive' features in the spectral function. Here, we use tunneling spectroscopy to reveal this signature of Hund physics in Sr$_2$RuO$_4$. By combining density functional theory, dynamical mean-field theory, and continuum local density of states calculations, we show that the experimental features are in excellent agreement with theoretical predictions and intimately linked to the non-monotonous energy dependence of the real part of the self-energy in a Hund metal. Our results provide direct experimental evidence for Hund-induced spectroscopic features and open a new route to probing correlation effects in quantum materials.
summary: This paper demonstrates that Hund coupling in Sr2RuO4 creates a unique 'superdispersion' feature where band velocity reverses direction. By using tunneling spectroscopy to probe unoccupied states, the authors successfully match experimental conductance dips with theoretical predictions of non-monotonic self-energy. This provides a new experimental route to identifying Hund physics in multi-orbital materials.
main_problem: The challenge of experimentally identifying and verifying the unique spectroscopic signature of Hund superdispersion in multi-orbital Hund metals, which is difficult to observe via ARPES due to its presence in unoccupied states.
main_result: The authors provide direct experimental evidence for Hund-induced superdispersion in Sr2RuO4 using tunneling spectroscopy, showing that Hund coupling induces a non-monotonic self-energy that causes band velocity to reverse direction.
method: The study combines experimental STM/STS and ARPES with a theoretical framework involving DFT, DMFT (using NRG solver), and continuum local density of states (cLDOS) calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system is the Hund metal Sr2RuO4, modeled using a multi-orbital (t2g) tight-binding Hamiltonian that incorporates Coulomb repulsion (U), Hund coupling (J), and spin-orbit coupling (SOC).
key_observables: Differential conductance (dI/dV), tunneling spectra (Feenstra function L(V)), quasiparticle interference (QPI) patterns, and the real and imaginary parts of the self-energy.
important_parameters: Hund coupling (J = 0.4 eV), Hubbard repulsion (U = 2.3 eV), spin-orbit coupling (lambda approx 200 meV), and energy scales of superdispersion (approx 160 meV).
main_assumptions: The bulk self-energy is assumed to be applicable to the surface electronic structure despite surface reconstruction (octahedral rotations).
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares waterfall vs. superdispersion schematics and self-energy components; Figure 2 shows surface vs. bulk structures and DFT band structures; Figure 4 presents spectral functions and simulated tunneling spectra (Feenstra function) showing the 160 meV dip.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the distinction between Mott and Hund physics, describes the experimental STM/ARPES setup and theoretical DFT+DMFT framework, presents the identification of superdispersion in the unoccupied states, and validates the model through comparison of simulated and experimental tunneling spectra.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Waterfalls and superdispersive spectral features. (a) Schematic of a waterfall (left), where vertical waterfall-like features connect the quasiparticle band with the Hubbard band, and the superdispersion found in a Hund metal (right), where a reversal of the dispersion originates from successive renormalization and ‘unrenormalization’ of the non-interacting bands. (b,d) Diagonal elements of the real (Σ′ mm(ω)) and imaginary (Σ′′ mm(ω)) parts of the self-energy for Sr2RuO4. In (b), we take (U, J) = (4.5, 0) eV, chosen to produce similar quasiparticle weights as for the physical case (U, J) = (2.3, 0.4) eV in (d). (c,e) Spectral functions of bulk Sr2RuO4 along the Γ-M-X-Γ path...

FIG. 2. Extracting the self-energy at the surface of Sr2RuO4. (a) ab-plane projection of Sr2RuO4 for the bulk unrotated structure (left) and surface rotated structure (right). The surface has a 9◦rotation of the RuO6 octahedral (grey squares) compared to the bulk. (b) Band structure of the 9◦rotated surface of Sr2RuO4 from DFT, with dominant orbital characters shown by red, green and blue for the dxz, dyz and dxy orbitals, respectively. The thin grey bands indicate the electronic structure of the unrotated two-atom unit cell of Sr2RuO4 (equivalent to the bulk structure) for comparison. (c) Comparison of ARPES data points (orange crosses) around the M point, extracted from Ref. 24, 25, with...

FIG. 3. Correlation-enhanced spin-orbit coupling at the surface of Sr2RuO4. (a) Com- parison of the experimental QPI (left-hand side, taken from Ref. 32) with cQPI+DMFT calculations (right-hand side) at 4.2 mV bias. (b) Energy dispersion along the qy = 0 line shown in a large energy range, revealing the modification of the scattering intensity due to self-energy effects with significant broadening away from the Fermi energy. (c-g) Closeup of the blue box in (b) for models calculated with different strengths of the spin-orbit coupling λ (specified in the lower right corner). A hybridization gap opens around the Fermi level producing two split branches of the dispersion, as seen in experiment...

FIG. 4. Signatures of Hund superdispersion in tunneling spectroscopy. (a, b) Spectral function of the surface of Sr2RuO4 when incorporating the DMFT bulk self-energy obtained with (a) artificial parameters (U, J) = (4.5, 0) eV and (b) the physical parameters (U, J) = (2.3, 0.4) eV. The parameters in (a) are chosen to produce similar quasiparticles weights as in (b). The difference between the two spectral functions demonstrates the influence of Hund coupling J. The red overlaid lines are the solutions to Eq. (2) underlining the superdispersive feature at 160 meV in (b) with a change in curvature of the dispersion. (c, d) Simulated tunneling spectra L(V ) [Eq. 5] for (c) J = 0 and (d) J > 0....
